cards.grammarNote: كَانَ (kāna) is the past tense of 'to be'. It is a كَانَ-sister (فعل ناقص): it takes a nominative subject and an accusative predicate. The adjective لَذِيذًا is in the accusative (tanwin -an).
كَانَتْ (kānat) is the feminine form of كَانَ. The predicate adjective سَاخِنَةً agrees with the subject in gender (feminine) and is in the accusative case.

cards.front: She used to cook every day.
cards.grammarNote: كَانَ + present tense verb expresses habitual past ('used to'). كَانَتْ تَطْبُخُ = she used to cook / she would cook. The present tense verb following كَانَ does not change its form.
كُنْتُ (kuntu) = I was / I used to. The 1st person singular of كَانَ. Full conjugation: كَانَ (he was), كَانَتْ (she was), كُنْتُ (I was), كُنَّا (we were), كُنْتَ (you were, m.).

كَانُوا (kānū) = they were (masculine plural). The plural predicate adjective جَائِعِينَ is in the accusative plural. Note: for inanimate plural nouns, the singular feminine form is used: كَانَتِ الأَطْبَاقُ نَظِيفَةً (the plates were clean).

cards.front: The market used to be busy.

cards.grammarNote: هَلْ (hal) turns a statement into a yes/no question. It comes before the verb. هَلْ كَانَ...؟ = was there...? كَانَ هُنَاكَ (kāna hunāka) = there was (lit. 'was there').
